title: Data Analytics and Business Intelligence
summary: From data to information to knowledge: data quality, databases, data warehouses, business intelligence, and business analytics for decision-making.
content_html:
Data Analytics and Business Intelligence

In the digital age, data is often the most valuable asset. Understanding how to collect, store, and analyse data is fundamental to competitive advantage.

Data vs Information vs Knowledge

  • Data: Raw characters, numbers, or symbols (0s and 1s). Data alone is not useful.
  • Information: Data organised in some way -- with units, context, and structure. Information systems store and present data efficiently and usefully.
  • Knowledge: Information applied over time to understand patterns and make predictions.

Data Quality Dimensions

  • Completeness: Are any values missing?
  • Validity: Do entered values make sense?
  • Uniqueness: Is each transaction/entity represented only once?
  • Consistency: Does summary information align with detailed information?
  • Timeliness: Is the data current with respect to business requirements?
  • Accuracy: Are all values correct?

Relational Databases

A relational database is a collection of data items organised as formally-described tables. They describe entities, their attributes, and relationships. Data can be accessed and reassembled in many ways without reorganising the tables. Benefits: minimise data redundancy and inconsistency; data can be shared among all users.

Data Silos Problem

Data silos occur when data is isolated in separate systems. This leads to inconsistency, duplication, and poor decision-making. Databases and integrated systems solve the silo problem.

Data Warehouse and Data Mart

  • Data Warehouse: A large database storing historical data from multiple sources, designed for analytical reporting and decision support (not day-to-day transactions).
  • Data Mart: A subset of a data warehouse focused on a particular business area (e.g. sales, marketing).

Business Intelligence (BI) and Analytics

Business Intelligence: Using data, tools, and processes to help business leaders make better decisions.

Business Analytics: More advanced -- using statistical analysis and predictive modelling on large data sets to discover patterns and predict future outcomes. Goes beyond BI by forecasting what will happen, not just describing what has happened.

exercises.title: Data vs information
options: Storing it in a database
Organising it with context, structure, and meaning
Backing it up to the cloud
Encrypting it for security
exercises.answer: Organising it with context, structure, and meaning

exercises.title: Data quality: validity
exercises.prompt: The data quality dimension 'validity' refers to:
options: Whether any values are missing
Whether entered values make logical sense
Whether data is up to date
Whether the same entity appears only once
exercises.answer: Whether entered values make logical sense

exercises.title: Relational database benefit
exercises.prompt: A key benefit of relational databases over separate departmental data stores is:
options: They are always faster than flat files
Data redundancy and inconsistency are minimised and data can be shared
They require no technical staff to maintain
They automatically generate business reports
exercises.answer: Data redundancy and inconsistency are minimised and data can be shared

exercises.title: Data warehouse purpose
exercises.prompt: A data warehouse is primarily used for:
options: Day-to-day transaction processing
Storing only the most recent data
Analytical reporting and decision support using historical data from multiple sources
Backing up operational databases
exercises.answer: Analytical reporting and decision support using historical data from multiple sources

exercises.title: Business analytics vs BI
exercises.prompt: Business analytics differs from business intelligence in that it:
options: Only looks at historical data
Focuses solely on financial reporting
Uses statistical analysis and predictive modelling to forecast future outcomes
Replaces the need for databases
exercises.answer: Uses statistical analysis and predictive modelling to forecast future outcomes

exercises.title: Data silo problem
exercises.prompt: Data silos in an organisation cause:
options: Better data security
Faster reporting
Data inconsistency, duplication, and poor decision-making
Reduced need for databases
exercises.answer: Data inconsistency, duplication, and poor decision-making

exercises.title: Data mart
options: A small retail data store
A subset of a data warehouse focused on a particular business area
An operational database for daily transactions
A type of cloud storage service
exercises.answer: A subset of a data warehouse focused on a particular business area

exercises.title: Data quality: timeliness
options: Data is delivered quickly to end users
Data is current and up-to-date with respect to business requirements
Data is stored in the fastest database available
Reports are generated in real time
exercises.answer: Data is current and up-to-date with respect to business requirements
